summary: Paul Graham argues that content-based, Bayesian spam filtering can effectively stop spam because spammers cannot avoid conveying their message, and Bayesian methods accurately estimate spam probability from word evidence. Compared to rule-based filters, Bayesian filters reduce false positives, adapt to evolving spam, and become stronger when trained per user. Over time, effective filtering lowers spam response rates enough to make spamming uneconomic.
key_points: Content-based Bayesian filtering is the most promising anti-spam strategy because it targets the unavoidable message itself.
Rule-based filters quickly hit a wall: they miss the last few percent of spam and increase false positives when tightened.
Bayesian filters measure true probabilities, combining both spammy and innocent evidence to avoid false positives.
Per-user training makes filters more accurate and harder for spammers to game, since each inbox has distinct vocabulary.
As filters force spam to become less persuasive, response rates drop and spamming becomes unprofitable.
money_quotes: The Achilles heel of the spammers is their message.
For most users, missing legitimate email is an order of magnitude worse than receiving spam, so a filter that yields false positives is like an acne cure that carries a risk of death to the patient.
The Bayesian approach assigns an actual probability.
They'd have to make their mails indistinguishable from your ordinary mail.
But if we get good enough at filtering out spam, it will stop working, and the spammers will actually stop sending it.
key_insight: Bayesian, per-user content filtering can both beat evolving spam and erode its economics by forcing spammers to dilute their pitches.
